How can venues become genuinely accessible?

Suggested phrasal verbs
miss out on cultural life
Model answer · 125 words

They should begin with an accessibility audit involving people with different mobility, sensory and cognitive needs. Physical access includes entrances, toilets, seating and transport information; programme access may require a captioned performance, audio description or sign-language interpretation. Booking systems, prices and staff behaviour also matter. Venues should publish accurate information before a visit and create routes for feedback. Accessibility is not a final certification: needs and technologies change, so institutions must budget for maintenance, training and repeated review rather than a one-off adaptation. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

PART 3 · 7

Does digital culture widen participation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
join in with an arts activity
Model answer · 128 words

It can widen geographic access and help people constrained by disability, cost or caring responsibilities. Recorded collections also let users pause, translate or revisit material. However, digital cultural access depends on equipment, connectivity and discoverability, and dominant platforms may make already famous work easier to find. Online participation may also lack the shared atmosphere or local relationships of a live event. Digital provision is most inclusive when it complements physical venues, provides accessible formats and is designed with the intended users rather than added as an afterthought. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. Regular review matters because access conditions and local cultural needs change over time.

PART 3 · 8

Should cultural institutions represent local communities?

Suggested phrasal verbs
come along to a performance
Model answer · 124 words

They should be accountable to the places they serve, but representation should not mean reproducing a demographic checklist in every programme. Community curation and community-led programming can bring local knowledge into decisions, particularly when an institution holds contested histories. Professional curators still contribute research and artistic judgement. The key is to state which decisions are shared and compensate participants fairly. Representation becomes meaningful when it changes interpretation, access or resources, not when community members appear in publicity after the programme is complete. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

PART 3 · 9

What is the value of amateur creativity?

Suggested phrasal verbs
set funding aside for
Model answer · 125 words

Amateur creativity lets people make meaning, develop skill and participate socially without converting every activity into employment. Choirs, craft groups and local theatre can sustain relationships and traditions over many years. Amateur does not mean careless; participants may pursue demanding standards while valuing enjoyment or community more than income. Public policy should offer affordable space, teaching and opportunities to perform without presenting amateurs as cheap substitutes for professionals. A healthy local cultural ecology needs routes for informal, emerging and professional practice to coexist. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

PART 3 · 10

Can arts projects strengthen neighbourhoods?

Suggested phrasal verbs
hand decisions over to
Model answer · 128 words

They can when the process is rooted in local relationships. Creative placemaking may help residents express history, animate a shared space or build cooperation around a practical project. However, an attractive artwork cannot repair insecure housing or substitute for political power. It can even become part of marketing that raises rents. A responsible project begins with local priorities, pays contributors, protects access and evaluates who gains. The strongest community-defined outcome may be a durable network or new capacity, not the number of visitors at an opening event. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. Regular review matters because access conditions and local cultural needs change over time.

PART 3 · 11

How should cultural impact be evaluated?

Suggested phrasal verbs
draw new audiences in
Model answer · 123 words

Evaluation should start with the intended mechanism. If a programme claims to widen access, attendance and an audience-demographic profile are relevant; if it claims to build skill, repeated observation or participant work may be stronger. A cultural-impact evaluation should separate intrinsic cultural value from outcomes such as spending or wellbeing instead of forcing all benefits into one number. Where causal claims matter, a comparison or counterfactual attendance estimate may help. Evidence should be proportionate, transparent and capable of informing future practice. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

PART 3 · 12

Should unpopular art receive public money?

Suggested phrasal verbs
branch out beyond
Model answer · 121 words

Sometimes it should, because popularity and public value are not identical. New, difficult or minority work may require time to find an audience, and artistic risk-taking would disappear if every grant guaranteed immediate sales. Nevertheless, unpopularity alone is not evidence of merit. Panels should assess artistic purpose, capability and the place of a project within a wider funding-portfolio balance. Transparent reasons allow citizens to criticise a decision without giving government direct power to approve only comfortable or fashionable expression. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

PART 3 · 13

How can schools build cultural participation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
build outward from
Model answer · 122 words

Schools can provide repeated encounters rather than one exceptional trip. A strong arts education pathway combines making, discussion, visits and contact with a creative practitioner. Pupils need enough technical knowledge to develop confidence, while programmes should include varied traditions and contemporary work. Partnerships with libraries, venues and local artists can continue beyond school hours. Funding matters because cultural participation should not depend on parents’ income. Evaluation should examine which pupils take part and whether the experience leads to further opportunities. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

PART 3 · 14

What risks arise when culture depends on sponsorship?

Suggested phrasal verbs
speak up through
Model answer · 123 words

Sponsorship can provide useful resources, but dependence may narrow artistic choices or create reputational conflict. A sponsor may expect prominent branding, access to audiences or quiet avoidance of controversial work. Even without direct censorship, organisations can anticipate what funders prefer. Diversified income, written ethical criteria and curatorial independence reduce this risk. Public disclosure should explain the relationship and any conditions. The aim is not to reject all commercial support, but to prevent financial leverage from silently determining cultural content or access. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

PART 3 · 15

What would an inclusive cultural system look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
keep a tradition alive
Model answer · 118 words

It would combine strong independent art with many routes into participation. People could encounter work through schools, libraries, a neighbourhood venue, digital platforms and accessible major institutions. Funding would strengthen the local cultural ecology, protect artistic risk and publish its distribution. Communities would influence relevant decisions without being asked to replace professional artists or public responsibility. The system would measure who participates and which barriers remain, but it would also respect disagreement and intrinsic cultural value. Inclusion would mean real choice and agency, not one approved cultural experience for everyone. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it. People affected by the decision should also have a clear route to challenge it.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

All publicly funded museums should offer free admission. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
free admissioncultural-participation ratenon-attendance barrierconcessionary ticketaccessibility auditaudience-demographic profilecultural participationcultural entitlementcultural democracy
Model essay · 314 words

Museums supported by taxpayers have a strong obligation to widen access, and free admission can remove an obvious barrier. I agree that permanent collections should normally be free where stable public funding covers the cost, but institutions should retain carefully designed charges for some temporary exhibitions if concessions and free routes remain available.

Free entry encourages spontaneous and repeat visits. People can explore one room without feeling pressure to justify an expensive ticket, and families may return as children’s interests change. It also avoids the stigma of means testing. These conditions can raise the cultural-participation rate and make a museum feel like a public space rather than a tourist product. Published evidence would make the funding choice easier for citizens and applicants to understand. This balance protects artistic purpose while making a publicly supported institution answerable for practical barriers.

However, admission income may support conservation, staff and ambitious exhibitions. Removing it without replacing the revenue could reduce opening hours or quality, which would not improve access. Price is also only one non-attendance barrier: transport, inaccessible information and unfamiliarity may keep people away even when entry costs nothing. Consultation should include people who do not currently attend, not only the institution’s existing audience.

Governments should therefore fund free permanent access while allowing transparent charges for unusually expensive temporary shows. A concessionary ticket, school provision and allocated free sessions can protect lower-income visitors. Museums should conduct an accessibility audit and compare the audience-demographic profile before and after the change, then invest in outreach where free entry alone has little effect. The measure should be reviewed over time because a short increase in attendance may not build lasting participation.

In conclusion, free admission should be the norm for publicly funded collections, but it requires replacement funding and complementary access measures. The goal is not a zero ticket price in isolation; it is meaningful and sustainable public use.

TASK 2 · 2

The growth of digital culture brings more advantages than disadvantages. Do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
digital cultural accessreach out beyondeveryday creativityliving cultural practicemobile arts serviceneighbourhood venuecultural participationcultural entitlementcultural democracy
Model essay · 315 words

Streaming, online collections and creative platforms have expanded the ways people encounter culture. I agree that the advantages outweigh the disadvantages because digital provision can widen reach and support new forms, although unequal connectivity and platform power require deliberate correction.

The clearest benefit is digital cultural access. A recorded performance or virtual collection can reach people limited by distance, disability, cost or caring responsibilities. Users may pause, enlarge, translate and revisit material, which can make interpretation easier. A local artist can also reach out beyond a nearby audience without first securing an expensive physical venue. Published evidence would make the funding choice easier for citizens and applicants to understand. This balance protects artistic purpose while making a publicly supported institution answerable for practical barriers.

Digital culture also supports creation. Affordable tools allow people to publish music, images and stories, strengthening everyday creativity. Online communities can sustain a living cultural practice across distance. These opportunities do not eliminate professional skill, but they reduce some institutional barriers to sharing work and finding collaborators. Consultation should include people who do not currently attend, not only the institution’s existing audience. A diverse funding portfolio can pursue more than one legitimate cultural objective at the same time.

The disadvantages are significant. Devices, broadband and digital confidence are unequal, while dominant platforms shape visibility and may reward familiar content. Recorded access can also become an excuse to withdraw a mobile arts service or neighbourhood venue. Public institutions should provide accessible formats, preserve local physical provision and explain how recommendation systems or rights restrictions affect discovery. The measure should be reviewed over time because a short increase in attendance may not build lasting participation.

Overall, digital culture extends participation more than it diminishes it, provided it complements rather than replaces shared cultural spaces. Its value depends on access, discoverability and fair conditions for creators, not merely the quantity of material placed online.

TASK 2 · 3

Cultural institutions should prioritise community participation over professional artistic excellence. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Optional collocation bank
curatorial independenceartistic risk-takinginstitutional gatekeepingcommunity curationparticipatory arts practiceparticipatory governance modelcommunity-defined outcomecultural participationcultural entitlement
Model essay · 304 words

Publicly supported cultural institutions are asked both to produce excellent work and to involve the communities they serve. Treating these aims as a simple hierarchy is mistaken. Professional ambition should remain central, but community participation can strengthen relevance, knowledge and access when it has a clearly defined role.

Those who prioritise excellence argue that artists and curators develop specialist judgement through years of practice. If every decision seeks immediate approval, institutions may avoid difficult work and weaken curatorial independence. Public funding should leave room for artistic risk-taking, including projects that initially attract a small or uncertain audience. Published evidence would make the funding choice easier for citizens and applicants to understand. This balance protects artistic purpose while making a publicly supported institution answerable for practical barriers.

Supporters of participation respond that professional institutions can reproduce institutional gatekeeping. Community curation may reveal histories or meanings that experts overlook, while participatory arts practice lets people create rather than remain passive consumers. Shared decision-making can also build trust where a venue has long appeared remote from local life. Consultation should include people who do not currently attend, not only the institution’s existing audience.

The best model distinguishes decisions instead of choosing one absolute priority. Professionals can retain responsibility for artistic standards, while a participatory governance model gives residents real influence over relevant collections, access and local programming. Roles, payment and final authority should be explicit. Evaluation can consider both artistic quality and a community-defined outcome without forcing them into one score. The measure should be reviewed over time because a short increase in attendance may not build lasting participation.

In conclusion, professional excellence and participation protect different aspects of public value. Institutions should pursue ambitious art while sharing power where community knowledge is essential. Neither token consultation nor popularity-driven programming is an adequate substitute for that balance.

TASK 2 · 4

In many societies, cultural participation is much higher among affluent groups. What causes this inequality, and what measures could reduce it?

Optional collocation bank
participation inequalitygeographic accesscultural access gapschool matineemobile arts serviceconcessionary ticketaudience-demographic profilecultural participationcultural entitlement
Model essay · 306 words

Affluent groups often participate more in formal cultural institutions because economic, geographic and educational advantages reinforce one another. Reducing this participation inequality requires more than cheaper tickets: policy must address early exposure, transport, accessibility and whether communities have meaningful influence over provision.

Cost is an important cause. Tickets, travel, food and childcare can make attendance expensive, while irregular work leaves little predictable leisure time. Cultural venues may also be concentrated in prosperous city centres, creating weak geographic access for peripheral communities. These practical barriers accumulate even when an individual is interested. Published evidence would make the funding choice easier for citizens and applicants to understand. This balance protects artistic purpose while making a publicly supported institution answerable for practical barriers.

Confidence and familiarity matter too. People exposed to museums, theatre or music through family and school learn how these institutions work. Others may perceive formal codes or programmes as irrelevant. If leadership and collections represent a narrow social group, the cultural access gap becomes symbolic as well as financial. Consultation should include people who do not currently attend, not only the institution’s existing audience. A diverse funding portfolio can pursue more than one legitimate cultural objective at the same time.

Governments should support a school matinee, sustained arts education, local venues and a mobile arts service. Institutions can offer concessionary ticket schemes, accessible information and community partnerships. An audience-demographic profile should be combined with interviews among non-attenders, and funding should reward evidence of barrier removal without demanding that every organisation reach every group. The measure should be reviewed over time because a short increase in attendance may not build lasting participation.

In conclusion, unequal participation is produced by connected material and institutional conditions rather than a simple lack of interest. Long-term local infrastructure, education and targeted access measures can widen opportunity while preserving diverse cultural choices.

TASK 2 · 5

Public arts funding should favour local and emerging artists rather than established national institutions. Discuss both views and give your own opinion.

Optional collocation bank
emerging artistfunding-portfolio balancecultural infrastructurelocal artiststudio spacelocal cultural ecologyoperating subsidycultural participationcultural entitlement
Model essay · 310 words

Large national institutions preserve collections, employ specialists and present work at a scale that small groups cannot match. Local and emerging artist support, however, is essential for renewal and geographic fairness. Public funding should not favour either category absolutely; it should maintain a transparent funding-portfolio balance that connects national capacity with local development.

Established institutions can provide conservation, research, major productions and broad educational resources. Their buildings and staff constitute valuable cultural infrastructure, and abrupt cuts may destroy capacity that is expensive to rebuild. They can also lend expertise and collections to regional partners, creating benefits beyond their immediate audiences. Published evidence would make the funding choice easier for citizens and applicants to understand. This balance protects artistic purpose while making a publicly supported institution answerable for practical barriers.

Nevertheless, prestige can become self-reinforcing. Organisations with experienced fundraisers repeatedly win grants, while a local artist lacks time, networks or affordable studio space. Concentrating support in capital cities weakens the local cultural ecology and limits which experiences reach public recognition. Smaller awards may produce substantial capacity when they include mentoring and core costs. Consultation should include people who do not currently attend, not only the institution’s existing audience.

Funding bodies should publish regional and organisational distributions, simplify applications and reserve suitable streams for emerging practice. National institutions should demonstrate partnerships, access and responsible collection care, while local decisions include community knowledge. Multi-year operating subsidy can support both scales when objectives are clear. Quality assessment should remain rigorous without treating existing reputation as its only proxy. The measure should be reviewed over time because a short increase in attendance may not build lasting participation.

In conclusion, national institutions and local artists perform complementary functions. A balanced portfolio should protect major public assets while creating genuine routes for new work and underserved places, making renewal part of cultural stewardship rather than its competitor.
